<content>
1: # Digital Marketing Fundamentals
2: 
3: ## Definition
4: 
5: Digital marketing is the promotion of products, services, or brands through
6: internet-connected channels and technologies — search engines, social media,
7: email, websites, and mobile apps. The American Marketing Association defines it
8: broadly as "any marketing methods conducted through electronic devices which
9: utilize some form of a computer."
10: 
11: The defining difference from traditional marketing is **measurability**: nearly
12: every click, open, and purchase is trackable, turning marketing from guesswork
13: into a discipline.
14: 
15: ## The durable fundamentals (they outlast tactics)
16: 
17: 1. **Core channels** — SEO, content, social, email, paid ads, affiliate/influencer.
18: 2. **The owned / earned / paid media model.**
19: 3. **A data & measurement loop.**
20: 4. **The customer funnel applied across digital touchpoints.**
21: 
22: Tools and platforms change; these foundations don't.
23: 
24: ## The core channels
25: 
26: | Channel | What it does | Character |
27: | --- | --- | --- |
28: | SEO | Earn organic visibility when people search | Durable, high-trust, compounds (3–6+ mo) |
29: | Content marketing | Attract by being useful, not interruptive | Compounds; fuels other channels |
30: | Social media | Build audience, community, brand | Discovery + relationship |
31: | Email | Nurture an owned audience directly | Highest ROI (~$36–42 per $1) |
32: | Paid ads / PPC | Buy attention & traffic | Fast, controllable; stops with budget |
33: | Affiliate / influencer | Reach via partners' borrowed trust | Leverage others' audiences |
34: 
35: ## Owned, Earned, Paid media
36: 
37: - **Owned** — your website, blog, email list, app. You control them; they compound.
38: - **Earned** — press, shares, reviews, word of mouth. Can't buy directly; most credible.
39: - **Paid** — ads across search/social/display. Fast and controllable but rented.
40: 
41: A strong strategy balances all three: paid rents demand, owned builds assets,
42: earned grants credibility.
43: 
44: ## The data & measurement loop
45: 
46: - Visibility into what works lets you find and fix drop-off points.
47: - Modern principle: **"measure what matters, respect the customer, act on the
48:   data you can ethically collect"** — not "track everything" (privacy changes,
49:   cookie restrictions, regulation).
50: - Metrics that matter: CAC (customer acquisition cost), ROAS (return on ad
51:   spend), CPL (cost per lead), CLV/LTV (lifetime value), conversion rate.
52: - ROI formula: `(Revenue - Marketing Cost) / Marketing Cost × 100`.
53: - Rule of thumb benchmarks cited: aim for ~3:1 ROAS on paid, ~5:1 on organic.
54: 
55: ## A starter framework
56: 
57: | Step | Focus | Why |
58: | --- | --- | --- |
59: | 1 | Fundamentals | Understand channels, media model, data, funnel |
60: | 2 | One channel | Build real competence in a single discipline |
61: | 3 | A real project | Apply with actual stakes and feedback |
62: | 4 | Analytics | Set up measurement to learn what works |
63: | 5 | Integration | Add channels and connect them into one system |
64: 
65: ## 8-step strategy framework
66: 
67: 1. Define business objectives & SMART marketing goals.
68: 2. Understand your audience deeply.
69: 3. Audit your current digital presence.
70: 4. Select your channel mix.
71: 5. Develop your content strategy.
72: 6. Set budget and allocate resources.
73: 7. Implement tracking & attribution.
74: 8. Launch, measure, and optimize.
75: 
76: ## What's changing in 2026 (AI era)
77: 
78: - Discovery is going **visual and conversational**: Google AI Overviews (~16% of
79:   queries), ChatGPT search, Perplexity, Gemini, and platform-native search
80:   (TikTok, YouTube, Reddit).
81: - New optimization layers: **AEO** (Answer Engine Optimization — get cited by AI
82:   engines) and **GEO** (Generative Engine Optimization — content shaped for AI
83:   synthesis).
84: - Privacy-first marketing and first-party data ownership are now essential.
85: - The fundamentals stay the same; execution becomes faster, more personalized,
86:   more visual.
87: 
88: ## Typical result timelines
89: 
90: - Paid (PPC/social ads): leads within 24–48 hours.
91: - Social organic: engagement in days, conversion tuning 2–4 weeks.
92: - SEO: meaningful movement in 3–6 months, big gains 6–12 months.
93: - Email (existing list): immediate.
94: 
95: ## Sources
96: 
97: - What is digital marketing? — Adobe: https://business.adobe.com/blog/basics/digital-marketing
98: - Digital Marketing Complete Guide (2026) — PxlPeak: https://pxlpeak.com/guide/digital-marketing-complete-guide
99: - Fundamentals of Digital Marketing (2026) — BrandClickX: https://brandclickx.com/fundamentals-of-digital-marketing/
100: - Digital Marketing: The Complete 2026 Guide — Frostbite: https://frostbitemarketing.com/resources/guides/digital-marketing-guide-2026/

102: - Ultimate Guide to Digital Marketing (PDF) — DigitalMarketer: https://www.digitalmarketer.com/digital-marketing/assets/pdf/ultimate-guide-to-digital-marketing.pdf

# Digital Marketing Fundamentals

## Definition

Digital marketing is the promotion of products, services, or brands through
internet-connected channels and technologies — search engines, social media,
email, websites, and mobile apps. The American Marketing Association defines it
broadly as "any marketing methods conducted through electronic devices which
utilize some form of a computer."

The defining difference from traditional marketing is **measurability**: nearly
every click, open, and purchase is trackable, turning marketing from guesswork
into a discipline.

## The durable fundamentals (they outlast tactics)

1. **Core channels** — SEO, content, social, email, paid ads, affiliate/influencer.
2. **The owned / earned / paid media model.**
3. **A data & measurement loop.**
4. **The customer funnel applied across digital touchpoints.**

1: # SEO (Search Engine Optimization)
2: 
3: Google-first reference, based on Google Search Central documentation plus current
4: 2026 guidance (AEO/GEO for AI search).
5: 
6: ## What SEO is
7: 
8: The practice of improving your website's content, structure, and authority so it
9: ranks higher in **unpaid (organic) search results**. One of the highest-ROI
10: channels because traffic is "free" once rankings are achieved, and it compounds.
11: 
12: ## How Google Search works (crawl → index → serve)
13: 
14: - **Googlebot** discovers URLs by crawling links, sitemaps, and redirects.
15: - Pages are **indexed** (added to the index) if reachable and not blocked.
16: - Google **serves** relevant results to queries.
17: 
18: Key implication: if Googlebot can't find or understand a page, it can't rank.
19: 
20: ## Essentials (Google Search Essentials)
21: 
22: - Create **helpful, reliable, people-first content**.
23: - Use words people actually search for, and place them naturally on the page.
24: - Make every page crawlable and indexable.
25: - Avoid deceptive or manipulative practices (cloaking, hidden text, link schemes).
26: 
27: ## Beginner checklist (SEO Starter Guide)
28: 
29: 1. **Descriptive page titles & meta descriptions** — unique per page; help users
30:    see relevance in results and can lift click-through.
31: 2. **Use words people search for** in headings and body copy.
32: 3. **Clear, logical site structure** — link important URLs together with
33:    descriptive anchor text.
34: 4. **Images**: use descriptive filenames and `alt` text.
35: 5. **Links**: build genuine, relevant links; use `rel="nofollow"` for paid/
36:    untrusted/required-login links.
37: 6. **Duplicate content**: consolidate with canonical tags; pick one canonical URL.
38: 7. **Mobile-friendly & fast**: Google uses a mobile crawler by default. Core Web
39:    Vitals matter.
40: 8. **Secure (HTTPS)**: preferred and a ranking/trust signal.
41: 9. **Videos**: provide text context; Googlebot can't read text inside video.
42: 
43: ## Developer / technical SEO
44: 
45: - Ensure all pages are reachable by an **anchor link** from another findable page.
46: - Submit an XML **sitemap** to encourage/smaritize crawling of key pages.
47: - For JS apps with one HTML page, give **each screen/content piece its own URL**.
48: - Keep key info in **text, not graphics**; use **semantic HTML**.
49: - Make content available in the **DOM** (CSS `content` property is not indexed).
50: - **robots.txt** blocks crawling; `noindex` blocks indexing but allows crawling.
51: - Use **canonical** tags to manage duplicate URLs.
52: - Use **301** redirects for permanent moves, **302** for temporary.
53: - Add **structured data** (schema) to enable rich results.
54: - Use URL Inspection / Rich Results Test to see how Google views your site.
55: - Manage crawl budget for very large sites via sitemaps + robots.txt.
56: 
57: ## 2026 evolution: AEO & GEO
58: 
59: - **AEO (Answer Engine Optimization)** — get cited by ChatGPT, Perplexity,
60:   Gemini. Tactics: structured data, topic authority, `llms.txt` directives.
61: - **GEO (Generative Engine Optimization)** — shape content for AI synthesis:
62:   clear topic sentences, entity-rich phrasing, strong source-quality signals.
63: - ~45% of B2B buyers now use AI for initial research. Treat AI engines as a
64:   distribution channel, not a trend.
65: 
66: ## Local SEO
67: 
68: - **Google Business Profile** wins the map pack; critical for service-area
69:   businesses.
70: - Build citations + reviews; keep NAP (name/address/phone) consistent.
71: 
72: ## Timelines
73: 
74: - Traditional SEO: meaningful results in 3–6 months, compounding gains 6–18 months.
75: - Google Business Profile map-pack movement: ~3–8 weeks.
76: - AEO/structured-data citation: ~4–10 weeks.
77: 
78: ## Measurement
79: 
80: - Google Search Console (indexing, queries, clicks, Core Web Vitals).
81: - Google Analytics 4 (engagement, conversions).
82: - Track rankings, impressions, CTR, organic conversions, and now AI visibility.
83: 
84: ## Sources
85: 
86: - SEO Starter Guide — Google Search Central: https://developers.google.com/search/docs/fundamentals/seo-starter-guide
87: - Google Search Essentials (guidelines): https://developers.google.com/search/docs/essentials
88: - SEO documentation hub: https://developers.google.com/search/docs
89: - SEO Guide for Web Developers: https://developers.google.com/search/docs/fundamentals/get-started-developers
90: - Technical SEO techniques: https://developers.google.com/search/docs/fundamentals/get-started
91: - SEO Starter Guide makeover (what changed): https://developers.google.com/search/blog/2024/02/ssg-gets-a-makeover

(End of file - total 91 lines)
</content>

1: # Email Marketing
2: 
3: Focus: deliverability and compliance (getting to the inbox), list health, and
4: best practices. Email remains one of the highest-ROI channels (~$36–42 per $1
5: spent).
6: 
7: ## Why deliverability matters
8: 
9: Great content is worthless if it lands in spam. Inbox placement depends on
10: **sender reputation**, authentication, list quality, sending behavior, and
11: engagement signals.
12: 
13: ## Authentication (mandatory)
14: 
15: Set up all three DNS-based standards:
16: 
17: - **SPF (Sender Policy Framework)** — authorizes which servers may send for your
18:   domain. Keep the record updated; don't exceed **10 DNS lookups** (SPF auto-fails).
19: - **DKIM (DomainKeys Identified Mail)** — cryptographic signature proving the
20:   domain owner sent the message. Use 1024- or 2048-bit keys.
21: - **DMARC (Domain-Based Message Authentication, Reporting & Conformance)** —
22:   tells receivers what to do on auth failure. Start at `p=none` (monitor), then
23:   move to `p=quarantine` or `p=reject`. Requires **domain alignment**: the `From`
24:   domain must match the one passing SPF or DKIM.
25: - **BIMI (Brand Indicators for Message Identification)** — shows your logo in
26:   inbox; requires DMARC `p=quarantine`/`reject` + a VMC (Verified Mark Certificate).
27: 
28: Gmail requirement (Feb 1, 2024): senders of **>5,000 messages/day** to Gmail must
29: have SPF + DKIM + DMARC, valid forward/reverse DNS (PTR), TLS, and **one-click
30: unsubscribe**.
31: 
32: ## Sender reputation & monitoring
33: 
34: - Use **dedicated vs shared IP** intentionally; shared IPs tie your fate to others.
35: - Monitor with Google Postmaster Tools, Sender Score, and your ESP dashboards.
36: - Keep **spam rate below 0.10%**, never reaching 0.30% (Gmail/Yahoo guidance).
37: - A single spam-trap hit can take 6–12 months to recover from.
38: 
39: ## List health (permission-first)
40: 
41: - Use **opt-in**; double opt-in is best practice to block invalid signups.
42: - **Never buy lists** — they're full of unengaged contacts and tank deliverability.
43: - Prune invalid/bounced/unsubscribed addresses; verify emails.
44: - Segment by interest/behavior for relevance.
45: - Honor unsubscribes within **48 hours**; one-click unsubscribe is required for
46:   bulk senders.
47: - Suppress spam complainers automatically.
48: 
49: ## Engagement benchmarks (Klaviyo reference)
50: 
51: - Open rate ≥ 33% (note: opens are less reliable post Apple MPP).
52: - Click rate ≥ 1%.
53: - Bounce rate < 1.0%.
54: - Unsubscribe rate < 0.3%.
55: 
56: Engagement (opens, replies, clicks) directly influences inbox placement.
57: 
58: ## Content & formatting
59: 
60: - **Subject lines**: honest, no ALL CAPS, no spammy words/excessive punctuation,
61:   avoid clickbait. Use 3–5 words + strong preview text.
62: - **HTML**: clean, well-formed; include a **plain-text version**; balance
63:   text-to-image ratio (don't send one giant image). Keep email < ~100KB.
64: - **Images**: add alt text; host on trusted domains/CDNs.
65: - **Links**: use your own domain or ESP-tracked HTTPS links; avoid URL
66:   shorteners (flagged by spam filters); check links for blacklisting/malware.
67: - Include physical mailing address in footer + visible unsubscribe (CAN-SPAM,
68:   GDPR, etc.).
69: 
70: ## Sending practices
71: 
72: - **Warm up** new/cold IPs and new domains (esp. <30 days old) gradually.
73: - Start low (e.g., 1–2K/day per provider), ramp slowly as engagement stays solid.
74: - Send at a **consistent cadence**; avoid big bursts after silence.
75: - Monitor SMTP responses (e.g., `4.7.28` / `421` = slow down).
76: - Target engaged users first to build reputation.
77: 
78: ## Compliance
79: 
80: - **CAN-SPAM (US)**: no deceptive headers/subjects, opt-out honored, physical
81:   address, no pre-checked boxes.
82: - **GDPR (EU)**: lawful basis + easy withdrawal; honor regional rules.
83: - **Gmail/Yahoo bulk sender rules** (2024): apply to any sender >5k/day.
84: 
85: ## Sources
86: 
87: - Gmail Email sender guidelines: https://support.google.com/mail/answer/81126
88: - Email Deliverability Checklist (2026) — WordStream: https://www.wordstream.com/blog/email-deliverability-checklist
89: - Email deliverability best practices — Klaviyo: https://help.klaviyo.com/hc/en-us/articles/25620771311643
90: - Email deliverability best practice guide — Adobe: https://business.adobe.com/resources/sdk/adobe-experience-platform-email-deliverability-best-practice-guide.html
91: - 2025 Marketer's Guide to Email Deliverability — Litmus: https://www.litmus.com/blog/why-email-deliverability-matters
92: - What is Email Deliverability? — Salesforce: https://www.salesforce.com/marketing/email/deliverability/

(End of file - total 92 lines)
</content>

1: # Social Media Marketing
2: 
3: SMM = using social platforms to connect with your audience to build brand,
4: increase sales, and drive traffic — via publishing, engagement, listening, and
5: paid social ads.
6: 
7: ## The #1 principle: focus, don't spray
8: 
9: - **Pick 1–2 primary platforms** where your audience actually spends time. A
10:   dominant presence on 2 beats a mediocre presence on 5–6.
11: - Random acts of content lead to random results. Strategy first.
12: - Use the **"rule of two"**: one **discovery** platform (TikTok, Reels, Shorts,
13:   Pinterest) + one **relationship** platform (LinkedIn, Threads, Reddit, email).
14: 
15: ## The 7-step strategy cycle (Buffer)
16: 
17: 1. Set **SMART goals**.
18: 2. Define your **target audience**.
19: 3. Choose **content pillars** (repeatable themes).
20: 4. Choose your **platforms** (where audience is).
21: 5. Build a **content calendar**.
22: 6. **Analyze** performance.
23: 7. **Evaluate** and iterate (it's a flywheel, not a one-time plan).
24: 
25: ## Content pillars & ratio
26: 
27: - Most successful brands use a **70/30 value-to-promo** split (or 60/25/15:
28:   60% educational, 25% personal/brand, 15% promotional).
29: - A working pillar set = 4–6 pillars, each with a fixed output ratio, format, and
30:   owner. Example: 40% educate, 30% entertain, 20% inspire, 10% promote.
31: - **UGC (user-generated content)** can see ~8.7x higher engagement than
32:   brand-produced content in some verticals — audiences trust peers.
33: 
34: ## Platform notes (2026 benchmarks)
35: 
36: - **TikTok** — best organic reach (~5.3% engagement). Vertical 9:16, hook in
37:   first 2 sec, trending audio, authenticity over polish. Strong for under-35 B2C.
38: - **LinkedIn** — ~6.5% organic engagement; **personal posts get ~561% more reach
39:   than company pages**. Best for B2B. Long-form text, document carousels, native
40:   video, thoughtful comments.
41: - **Instagram** — Reels + carousels; strongest all-around for visual/e-commerce.
42: - **YouTube** — long-form builds authority; Shorts drive subscriber growth.
43: - **Facebook** — organic reach ~0.06% for pages (pay-to-play); use for groups,
44:   local ads, Marketplace.
45: - **Pinterest** — high-quality visual, aspirational/visual products.
46: 
47: ## Engagement is the real lever
48: 
49: - Replying to comments consistently raises engagement across platforms (up to
50:   +42% on Threads, +30% on LinkedIn per Buffer's 2026 report).
51: - Answer DMs < 60 min (business hours), comments < 2 hr.
52: - Treat DMs/comments as a product surface, not an afterthought.
53: 
54: ## Organic + paid = one system
55: 
56: - Pure organic plateaus; pure paid builds no equity. Use organic to find winners,
57:   paid to amplify them, creators to extend reach.
58: - **Whitelisted creator content** (ads run from the creator's handle) often
59:   outperforms brand creative 2–4x on CTR/CPA on Meta & TikTok.
60: - Typical mid-market split: 50% paid to cold, 20% retargeting, 20% creators,
61:   10% experimentation. Review quarterly vs blended CAC.
62: 
63: ## Measurement (3 layers)
64: 
65: - **Leading**: saves, shares, watch-time, DM volume, comment velocity, branded
66:   search. Predicts results 60–90 days out.
67: - **Lagging**: follower growth rate, reach %, engagement rate per pillar, top
68:   posts by format (report vs 90-day median, ignore viral spikes).
69: - **Business**: branded search growth, assisted conversions, social-sourced
70:   pipeline, CAC contribution, LTV differential. Report as a range; last-click
71:   undervalues social.
72: - **Server-side conversion APIs are mandatory** (Meta CAPI, TikTok Events API,
73:   LinkedIn Conversions API) — client-side pixels miss 30–60% post iOS 17 / ITP.
74:   Run geo-lift/holdout tests at least annually.
75: 
76: ## Beginner start
77: 
78: Pick one platform, set one clear goal, post useful content, respond to people,
79: measure what resonates, then expand. Progress > perfection.
80: 
81: ## Sources
82: 
83: - How to create a great social media strategy in 2026 — HubSpot: https://blog.hubspot.com/marketing/social-media-strategy-for-your-business
84: - Your Complete Guide to Social Media Marketing (2026) — Buffer: https://buffer.com/resources/social-media-marketing-guide/
85: - 7-Step Social Media Marketing Strategy (2026) — Buffer: https://buffer.com/resources/social-media-marketing-strategy/
86: - Social Media Marketing Strategy 2026 — Bear My Brand: https://bearmybrand.com/blog/social-media-marketing-strategy-2026
87: - Social Media Marketing: A 2026 Strategy Guide — 2POINT: https://www.2pointagency.com/guides/social-media-marketing-the-complete-2026-strategy-guide/

1: # Paid Ads / PPC (Pay-Per-Click)
2: 
3: Primary focus: **Google Ads** campaign structure & best practices, plus general
4: PPC principles. PPC buys targeted traffic fast; it stops when the budget stops,
5: so it pairs with (doesn't replace) organic.
6: 
7: ## What PPC is
8: 
9: You pay per click (or impression/action) to show ads on search engines, social
10: platforms, and display networks. Highest-intent traffic: people searching for
11: what you offer. Google Search Ads cited at ~4.4% avg conversion rate; organic
12: converts ~14.6% vs 1.7% for outbound.
13: 
14: ## Google Ads account structure
15: 
16: Top-down hierarchy:
17: 
18: ```
19: Account
20: └── Campaign        (budget, location, network, settings)
21:     └── Ad Group    (theme + related keywords + ads)
22:         └── Ads + Keywords
23: ```
24: 
25: **Best practices**
26: 
27: - Organize as you create; reorganizing later wipes accumulated data/learning.
28: - Create a **separate campaign** when you need different budget or location
29:   targeting (e.g., shirts vs pants).
30: - One campaign can share budget/location across ad groups.
31: - Each **ad group = one narrow theme** with tightly related keywords.
32: - Mention at least one keyword in the ad headline for relevance.
33: - Keep **≥3 ads per ad group** and use optimized ad rotation to let Google pick
34:   the best per auction (signals: keyword, search term, device, location).
35: 
36: ## Campaign types
37: 
38: Choose by objective:
39: 
40: - **Search** — text ads on search results (high intent).
41: - **Display** — image ads on websites.
42: - **Video** — video ads on YouTube.
43: - **Shopping** — product listings on Google.
44: - **Demand Gen** — ads within online feeds.
45: - **App** — promote your app.
46: - **Performance Max** — find high-value customers across all channels.
47: - **Local** — promote physical locations.
48: 
49: ## Setup flow
50: 
51: 1. Add business info (name, URL; link Business Profile / YouTube).
52: 2. Choose advertising **objective & goal** → Google recommends campaign type.
53: 3. Set budget & bidding.
54: 4. Enter payment details.
55: 
56: ## Core PPC mechanics to know
57: 
58: - **Keywords & match types** target searches; use negative keywords to cut waste.
59: - **Quality Score** (expected CTR, ad relevance, landing-page experience) drives
60:   cost & position — write relevant ads and send to strong landing pages.
61: - **Bidding**: manual or automated (e.g., maximize clicks/conversions, tCPA,
62:   tROAS). Start simple, then automate once data exists.
63: - **Conversion tracking** is mandatory — without it you can't optimize.
64: - **Search terms report** → refine keywords/negatives continuously.
65: 
66: ## Measurement & targets
67: 
68: - ROAS (return on ad spend), CAC, CPL, CPA, CTR, conversion rate.
69: - Rule of thumb: aim for **≥3:1 ROAS** on paid channels.
70: - Run paid for fast pipeline; use organic (SEO, email, Business Profile) for
71:   compounding CPA.
72: 
73: ## General PPC best practices (cross-platform)
74: 
75: - Set a clear budget and track every conversion.
76: - Test offers/creative in small batches; scale winners.
77: - Align ad → landing page message (consistency lifts conversion).
78: - Use retargeting to recapture interested users.
79: - Watch frequency to avoid ad fatigue.
80: 
81: ## Sources
82: 
83: - Organize your account with ad groups — Google Ads Help: https://support.google.com/google-ads/answer/6372655
84: - Google Ads Best Practices: https://support.google.com/google-ads/answer/6154846
85: - Create a campaign — Google Ads Help: https://support.google.com/google-ads/answer/6324971
86: - Campaigns overview — Google Ads API: https://developers.google.com/google-ads/api/docs/campaigns/overview
87: - Perfect Google Ads campaign structure (2026) — LeadsBridge: https://leadsbridge.com/blog/google-ads-campaign-structure/
88: - How campaign structure shapes performance — Search Engine Land: https://searchengineland.com/how-campaign-structure-shapes-google-ads-performance-481332

(End of file - total 88 lines)
</content>

1: # Content Marketing
2: 
3: Content marketing attracts, engages, and converts audiences through genuinely
4: useful content (articles, guides, video, infographics) rather than interruptive
5: ads. It feeds SEO, social, and email, and compounds over time.
6: 
7: ## Definition
8: 
9: A **documented plan** defining what content you'll create, for whom, through which
10: channels, and how you'll measure success — with every piece tied to a business
11: goal.
12: 
13: ## The 6-component framework (CMI / 2026 synthesis)
14: 
15: 1. **Strategic foundation** — goals/objectives (awareness, demand gen, pipeline,
16:    retention).
17: 2. **Audience intelligence** — for B2B, map the full **buying committee** (a VP
18:    and a CTO have different needs); refresh personas annually.
19: 3. **Content architecture** — 3–5 **content pillars** (buyer pain areas, not
20:    product categories) + topic clusters.
21: 4. **Production & editorial process** — editorial calendar (themes over time) +
22:    content calendar (per piece: keyword, persona, funnel stage, owner, date).
23: 5. **Distribution & promotion** — plan this *before* writing. Budget 25–35% of
24:    content spend on distribution for mature B2B.
25: 6. **Measurement & analytics** — KPIs before copy.
26: 
27: > Only **47% of B2B marketers have a documented strategy** (CMI, 2025). Only ~3%
28: > of your market is buying now; build for the 97% still researching.
29: 
30: ## Build it in 9 steps
31: 
32: 1. Define goals.
33: 2. Research audience (CRM data, sales calls, social analytics; map buying committee).
34: 3. Run a **content audit** (inventory; tag keep/update/consolidate/remove).
35: 4. Choose a CMS + governance model (who can publish, review, style).
36: 5. Determine content types & formats.
37: 6. Map content to the buyer journey (TOFU/MOFU/BOFU).
38: 7. Build the distribution engine (plan "where will this live?" first).
39: 8. Add the **AEO layer** (see below).
40: 9. Implement measurement from day one.
41: 
42: ## Funnel-stage content
43: 
44: - **TOFU (Awareness)**: educational blog, explainers, newsletters — identify problems.
45: - **MOFU (Consideration)**: guides, comparisons, webinars, case-study lite.
46: - **BOFU (Decision)**: customer success stories w/ quantified outcomes, security/
47:   compliance docs, implementation roadmaps, competitive displacement, pricing.
48: 
49: ## The AEO layer (2026)
50: 
51: ~45% of B2B buyers use AI for initial research; 67% prefer rep-free buying.
52: Tactics:
53: 
54: - Lead each page with a **direct declarative answer**.
55: - Build **entity density** (name specific tools, frameworks, companies).
56: - Make **H2 headings answer specific questions**.
57: - Treat **FAQ sections as strategic citation assets** for AI engines.
58: 
59: ## Distribution (B2B ranked by ICP precision)
60: 
61: Owned media (website, email, LinkedIn) first → niche industry media → webinar
62: co-hosting → content syndication → LinkedIn paid → community seeding →
63: partnerships. Add AEO as a mandatory layer.
64: 
65: ## Repurposing = extraction, not resizing
66: 
67: Mine a long-form source (e.g., a 45-min expert conversation) into: short video
68: clips, LinkedIn posts, newsletter snippets, FAQ sections, a blog post. This yields
69: **3–5x reach at 20–30% of net-new cost**.
70: 
71: ## Measurement (CMI 5-stage model)
72: 
73: 1. **See Us** — reach, traffic, AI visibility.
74: 2. **Connect** — engagement, time on page, email CTR.
75: 3. **Trust Us** — returning-visitor rate, webinar attendance (most teams skip trust).
76: 4. **Choose Us** — lead forms, content-assisted conversions.
77: 5. **Champion Us** — retention, referrals.
78: 
79: Add **human-input measurement** (advisory boards, win/loss interviews) — analytics
80: show content was *consumed*; conversations show it *mattered*.
81: 
82: ## Common mistakes
83: 
84: - Optimizing for traffic instead of pipeline.
85: - Publishing with no distribution plan.
86: - Building for one persona in a committee buying process.
87: - Ignoring AI search as a distribution channel.
88: - Scaling production before proving quality.
89: - Measuring only what's easy to measure.
90: 
91: ## Sources

# Content Marketing

Content marketing attracts, engages, and converts audiences through genuinely
useful content (articles, guides, video, infographics) rather than interruptive
ads. It feeds SEO, social, and email, and compounds over time.

## Definition

A **documented plan** defining what content you'll create, for whom, through which
channels, and how you'll measure success — with every piece tied to a business
goal.

## The 6-component framework (CMI / 2026 synthesis)

1. **Strategic foundation** — goals/objectives (awareness, demand gen, pipeline,
   retention).
2. **Audience intelligence** — for B2B, map the full **buying committee** (a VP
   and a CTO have different needs); refresh personas annually.
3. **Content architecture** — 3–5 **content pillars** (buyer pain areas, not
   product categories) + topic clusters.

+export const DIFFICULTY_WEIGHT: Record<Difficulty, number> = { 1: 1, 2: 1.5, 3: 2 };

+export function examMaxWeight(exam: Exam): number {

+  "attemptId,examId,examTitle,domain,candidate,email,percentage,weightedPercentage,band,passed,elapsedSeconds,timedOut,tabSwitches,takenAt";
+
+function csvField(value: string): string {
+  const needsQuoting = /[",\n\r]/.test(value);
+  if (!needsQuoting) return value;
+  return '"' + value.replace(/"/g, '""') + '"';
+}
+
+export function attemptToCsvRow(r: AttemptSummary): string {
+  return [
+    csvField(r.attemptId),

+    csvField(r.domain),

+    String(r.timedOut),
+    String(r.tabSwitches),
+    String(r.takenAt),
+  ].join(",");
+}
+
+// FNV-1a 32-bit → 8 hex chars. Deterministic for identical inputs.
+function fnv1aHex(input: string): string {
+  let hash = 0x811c9dc5;
+  for (let i = 0; i < input.length; i++) {
+    hash ^= input.charCodeAt(i);
+    // FNV prime multiplication, keep within 32-bit unsigned.
+    hash = Math.imul(hash, 0x01000193) >>> 0;
+  }
+  const hex = (hash >>> 0).toString(16);
+  return hex.padStart(8, "0").slice(0, 8);
+}
+
+export function certificateId(examId: string, candidate: string, takenAt: number): string {
+  const seed = `${examId}|${candidate}|${takenAt}`;
+  return `MKT-${fnv1aHex(seed)}`;
